issues.area: Naming drift — cross_repo_impact output shape (catalog vs ported struct vs HTTP/MCP/SDK)
issues.problem: The canonical catalog defines cross_repo_impact output rows as {repo_id, symbol, path, line, via, contract:{method,path}, confidence}. But the actual ported struct service.CrossRepoImpact (code_search_service.go:138) has NONE of those per-symbol fields — it is {ConsumerRepo, ViaRoutes[], CallingFiles[], Tests[], TestsTotal, TestsTruncated, Linked, Indexed, RiskLevel}. There is no symbol/path/line/via/contract/confidence on it. The Core spec (§2 graph.CrossDep, §8 CrossImpact) invents yet a THIRD shape {RepoID, Symbol, Path, Line, Via, Contract, Confidence}. The HTTP spec (§5.5) serializes a FOURTH shape mixing {consumer_repo, via_routes, via, contract, confidence, linked, indexed, risk_level, tests, tests_total, tests_truncated}. The MCP spec (§4.2) shows a FIFTH {repo_id, consumer_repo, via, contract, calling_files, confidence, linked, indexed, tests, tests_total, tests_truncated}. Four surfaces each claim to serialize 'CrossRepoImpact verbatim' but emit four different field sets. The HTTP spec even asserts 'do not redefine these shapes... already exist in the engine's response structs' which is false for via/contract/confidence/symbol/path/line.
issues.fix: Pick ONE canonical cross-repo row schema and define it once in pkg/apitypes (or pkg/atlas results). Either (a) extend the ported CrossRepoImpact struct to actually carry symbol/path/line/via/contract/confidence and update code_search_service.go, then have all surfaces serialize it, or (b) amend the catalog output to match the real struct (consumer_repo/via_routes/calling_files/linked/indexed/risk_level/tests/tests_total/tests_truncated) and drop the fictional per-symbol fields. Make every spec reference the single struct; delete the divergent inline JSON examples.

issues.area: Naming drift — repo identity field (repo_full_name vs repo_id vs FullName vs RepoID)
issues.problem: The repo identifier is named inconsistently across surfaces, which breaks the SDK's stated 'method-isomorphic, one-line swap' guarantee. Core §2 graph.Repo uses FullName + ID(uuid). HTTP §1 addresses by repo_id(uuid) OR repo(=repo_full_name). CLI uses --repo accepting path|basename|org/name|repo_id with sentinels 'all' and '.'. MCP uses repo_id (repo_<slug>, NOT a uuid) plus a separate 'repo' arg. The cross_repo_impact output alternately calls it origin_repo, consumer_repo, repo_id, and repo_full_name within the same op. An agent threading IDs across search→impact→cross_repo_impact cannot rely on a stable field name or even a stable ID format (uuid in HTTP, repo_<slug> in MCP).
issues.fix: Define one repo-addressing contract: a canonical repo_id string format used identically by every surface, and a single human alias field name (repo_full_name) used everywhere it appears in outputs. Document the resolution rules (path/basename/org-name → repo_id) once in the Core/config spec and have CLI/HTTP/MCP reference it. Stop emitting origin_repo/consumer_repo/repo as ad-hoc synonyms — use repo_id + a labeled role.

issues.area: Vectors-optional plumbing — search MCP tool diverges from catalog and CLI/HTTP
issues.problem: The locked decision #3 keeps vectors off the core path, but the surfaces implement the gate three incompatible ways. The catalog's search op explicitly takes mode (lexical|semantic|hybrid). CLI search keeps --mode lexical|semantic|hybrid (catalog-faithful). HTTP search keeps ?mode=lexical|semantic|hybrid (catalog-faithful). But the MCP spec (§4.1, §4.6) deliberately REMOVES the mode arg from `search` and splits semantic into a separate gated `semantic_search` tool that maps to a net-new Op 'semantic_search' which is NOT in the canonical catalog. So the same canonical op 'search' has a mode param on 3 surfaces and not on MCP, and MCP introduces an uncatalogued op. An SDK/agent cannot treat search uniformly.
issues.fix: Either add 'semantic_search' to the canonical catalog as a first-class op (and document that CLI/HTTP expose it via --mode while MCP exposes it as a distinct tool — an intentional, documented surface-shaping), OR keep search.mode everywhere and drop the separate MCP tool. Whichever: reconcile so the op set is identical across catalog + all surfaces, and state the mode↔tool mapping explicitly.

issues.area: Multi-tenancy gap — Core/CLI/MCP-local lack the tenant scoping that HTTP mandates
issues.problem: The HTTP spec makes TenantScope 'the security keystone' — every StorageDriver call MUST filter on tenant_id, enforced structurally. But the Core engine spec models tenancy only as an optional graph.Repo.Scope *uuid.UUID and a Config.Scope, with NO requirement that StorageDriver methods take/enforce a tenant. The StorageDriver interface (§3) passes scope *uuid.UUID to only SOME methods (ListRepos, FindRepo, ListProducerRoutes, ListCoverageForSymbols, service aliases) but NOT to the core reads (ListSymbols, ListEdges, GetSymbolsByIDs, LatestSnapshot, ListRoutes, ListCoverageForTest, jobs). The HTTP layer's promise that 'a missing tenant filter is structurally impossible' cannot hold if the driver methods it calls don't take a tenant. This is a cross-cutting consistency hole between the security spec and the keystone interface.
issues.fix: Decide tenancy enforcement boundary and make it consistent: either thread scope/tenant into EVERY StorageDriver read+write signature (so the filter is structurally unavoidable as HTTP claims), or scope at the snapshot/repo resolution layer and document that all snapshot_ids are already tenant-bound by construction. Update the StorageDriver interface so the set of tenant-carrying methods is complete and matches the HTTP security guarantee.

issues.area: Catalog mapping gap — index progress / async jobs / runs have no canonical op
issues.problem: HTTP and SDK expose first-class resources with no catalog op: GET /jobs/{job_id}, GET /jobs/{job_id}/events (SSE), GET /runs/{run_id}, GET /runs/{run_id}/events, DELETE /runs/{run_id}, POST /webhooks/github. The SDK exposes Job[T].Status/Await/Events/Cancel. The catalog's 'status' op is documented to poll runs ('--run' in CLI), but the catalog status output schema {tier,storage_driver,...repos[]} has no run/job polling fields, and there is no catalog entry for job-event streaming, run cancellation, or the webhook endpoint. So a load-bearing part of the agentic+index async lifecycle is unmapped to the canonical catalog the whole design claims to be 1:1 with.
issues.fix: Add canonical catalog entries (or explicitly document them as transport-only sub-resources of index/rca/fix/review/link): job (get/events/cancel), run (get/events/cancel), webhook. Extend the status op output to include the run/job poll payload the CLI/SDK rely on, or define a separate 'job_status' op. Make the async lifecycle explicit in the catalog so CLI --run, SDK Job[T], and HTTP /runs all map to a named op.

issues.area: Versioning inconsistency — graph.json schema_version vs API /v1 vs pkg SemVer not reconciled
issues.problem: Three independent version axes are defined but their relationship/values conflict. Core §2 ids/graph_export meta says schema_version with no value; CLI/MCP graph_export emit schema_version; Repo-layout §8.1 fixes SchemaVersion = 'atlas-1'; HTTP meta carries schema_version:'1.0'. So the same graph.json schema_version field is '1.0' (HTTP) vs 'atlas-1' (repo spec). Separately the HTTP API is /api/v1 while MCP advertises protocolVersion 2025-06-18 and SDK semver tracks 'API minor' — but there is no single statement of how a graphify-compat consumer detects schema compatibility.
issues.fix: Fix one literal value for graph.json schema_version (e.g. 'atlas-1') and use it in every spec's example. State the three version axes (graph schema, HTTP /vN, pkg SemVer, MCP protocolVersion) and their independence in one place (docs/architecture or config spec), and make the golden-graph test assert the literal.

issues.area: Error model inconsistency — RFC 9457 (HTTP) vs sentinel errors (SDK) vs degrade-not-error (MCP) vs exit codes (CLI) lack a shared code vocabulary
issues.problem: Each surface invents its own error taxonomy with no shared canonical code set. HTTP §8 defines codes {repo_not_indexed, tier_unavailable, vectors_disabled, insufficient_scope, invalid_cursor, agent_concurrency,...}. SDK §A.6 defines sentinels {ErrNotFound, ErrTierUnsupported, ErrNotIndexed, ErrVectorsDisabled, ErrAmbiguousSymbol}. MCP §0.5 returns degrade results {status:'not_indexed'|'unsupported_tier'|'vectors_disabled'} as isError:false. CLI §1.2 maps to numeric exit codes {3 not found,4 not indexed,5 storage,6 tier,8 degraded}. The names almost-but-not-quite align (not_indexed vs ErrNotIndexed vs repo_not_indexed; tier_unavailable vs ErrTierUnsupported vs unsupported_tier vs exit 6). A client SDK translating HTTP problem+json codes to typed errors has no canonical mapping, and CLI exit-code 8 'degraded' (UnlinkedDependency) has no corresponding HTTP code or SDK sentinel.
issues.fix: Define a single canonical error-code enum (string codes) in pkg/apitypes used by HTTP problem.code, mapped 1:1 to SDK sentinels, MCP degrade.status values, and CLI exit codes. Publish the mapping table (code → HTTP status → SDK error → MCP status → CLI exit) once. Ensure 'degraded/unlinked' is represented on every surface (HTTP currently has no degraded code; SDK has no ErrDegraded).

issues.area: Pagination inconsistency — cursor everywhere except impact/cross_repo_impact/tests_for_change, which truncate silently-ish
issues.problem: The design mandates cursor pagination (HTTP §6, MCP §3) but the large fan-out ops (impact, cross_repo_impact, tests_for_change) are NOT cursor-paginated — they bound by depth + per-level limit and report truncation via tests_truncated / meta.truncated. That is a reasonable choice, but it is stated inconsistently: HTTP cursor section says limits are per-op (search 20, refs 200, neighbors 100, callers 100) yet impact/tests_for_change have no documented page size or truncation field in their catalog outputs, while cross_repo_impact's tests_total/tests_truncated only cover the per-consumer test cap, not the impacted[] array cap. An agent cannot tell whether impacted_symbols[] or selected_tests[] was truncated.
issues.fix: For every list-returning op, document either a cursor or an explicit {total, returned, truncated} trio in its catalog output. Add truncation flags to impact (impacted_symbols/files/tests) and tests_for_change (selected_tests) outputs, mirroring cross_repo_impact's tests_truncated. State the cursor-vs-bounded-truncation policy per op-category once.

issues.area: Streaming gap — graph_export streaming contract differs across surfaces
issues.problem: graph_export streaming is described three incompatible ways. HTTP §2 says it 'can emit application/x-ndjson' and §11 says SSE support is weak so kin-openapi is used — but no SSE for export, just NDJSON. Core/CLI emit format graphjson|jsonl to --out/stdout. SDK §B.8 says 'chunked application/x-ndjson'. MCP §4.1/§6 says scope:full returns a RESOURCE LINK (atlas://graph/...) NOT inline, and only subgraph is inline. So the same op streams as: NDJSON body (HTTP/SDK), file blob (CLI), or a resource URI requiring a second resources/read (MCP). The 'format' enum is graphjson|jsonl in catalog/CLI/Core but the wire content-type is x-ndjson in HTTP/SDK — jsonl vs ndjson naming drift, and graphjson (a single document) cannot be 'streamed chunked' the way ndjson can.
issues.fix: Specify graph_export transport per surface explicitly and reconcile the format names (jsonl == application/x-ndjson; graphjson == single application/json document, buffered or chunked-but-whole). State that MCP returns a resource link for scope:full by design while HTTP/SDK stream the body, and align the format enum value used in examples (jsonl) with the content-type (x-ndjson).

issues.area: Observability cross-cutting — defined only in Repo-layout and Core, absent from HTTP/MCP/CLI surface contracts
issues.problem: Observability is a stated cross-cutting concern but is unevenly specified. Repo-layout has internal/observability (zap + prometheus, no-op local). Core §1 has WithLogger/WithMetrics. HTTP mounts /metrics (auth-gated) and a zap Logger middleware + X-Request-Id. But MCP only says 'log to stderr' with no metrics/request-id correlation, and CLI has -v/-vv to stderr with no request-id/trace propagation. There is no end-to-end request/trace id that ties a CLI invocation → HTTP call → engine op → job, despite HTTP generating X-Request-Id and SDK echoing it. No mention of OpenTelemetry/tracing anywhere, and metrics names/labels are never enumerated.
issues.fix: Add a short cross-cutting observability contract: a single request/trace-id propagated CLI→SDK→HTTP→engine→job (reuse HTTP X-Request-Id, surface it in CLI --json meta and MCP _meta), a named metrics set (index_duration, search_latency, queue_depth, impact_depth_reached) emitted by the engine regardless of surface, and state whether OTel tracing is in scope. Make MCP/CLI reference the same logger/metrics sink as HTTP.

issues.area: Catalog mapping gap — explain and graph_export missing from MCP/HTTP method-map for one tier, and 'link' local semantics inconsistent
issues.problem: Minor 1:1 mapping mismatches: (a) The catalog marks explain/graph_export/history/snapshot_diff/coverage/tests_for_change as tier:both, and MCP correctly serves them locally, but the SDK §A.2 Engine groups CrossRepoImpact/Consumers/RouteContracts as hosted-only while the catalog marks route_contracts implicitly via cross-repo (tier hosted) — consistent — yet the SDK comment says these 'return ErrTierUnsupported on local' while the Core §8 says 'Local tier stores routes/crossdeps but matching is single-repo only — degrades gracefully to honest empty, never fabricated.' So is local consumers/route_contracts an error (SDK) or an honest-empty (Core)? (b) link on local is described as 'no-op path-add returning linked:true,webhook_registered:false' (CLI/HTTP/MCP/SDK agree) but HTTP marks link tier H and returns 404 tier_unavailable for hosted-only routes on local — contradicting the 'local resolves to path-add' behavior.
issues.fix: Reconcile the local-tier behavior of the cross-repo/link ops: decide per op whether local returns ErrTierUnsupported, honest-empty, or a degraded no-op, and state it identically in Core/CLI/HTTP/MCP/SDK. Specifically fix link: if local does a path-add no-op it must NOT be a tier_unavailable 404 — carve it out of the hosted-only 404 rule.

issues.area: Parser / graph model — symbol-granular edges
issues.problem: The entire callers/path/impact/cross-repo story is specified at SYMBOL granularity (Edge{FromSymbol, ToRef, Kind:EdgeCalls}, ParseFileFull returning call edges, NodeID-keyed traversal), and the port manifest rates the parser port 'effort: S — no changes needed'. Ground truth contradicts this: aziron-pulse models.DependencyEdge (devops.go:242) has FromFile/ToRef/EdgeType ONLY — no from_symbol column, no caller-symbol. tree_sitter_parser.go extracts NO call edges at all (grep for call_expression/EdgeCalls/from_symbol returns nothing); it emits file-level import edges. So callers()/References()/Path()/Impact() at symbol resolution, the ambiguity guard, the cross-repo bridge edges, and the NodeID set-diff for history/snapshot_diff are all NET-NEW parser+model+storage work, not a port. This is the load-bearing capability of the whole product and it does not exist in the source.
issues.fix: Reclassify the parser/edge work from 'S/port' to 'L/new build'. Add a real call-graph extraction pass (second AST walk per function body collecting callee identifiers) with name->NodeID resolution, add FromSymbol/ToSymbol/Line columns to the edge model and BOTH schemas, and budget the call-resolution + overload disambiguation as the largest single work item. Do not ship the spec's effort table; it under-budgets the core by ~10x.

issues.area: Storage — NodeID stable identity vs actual delta keying
issues.problem: Section 2/3 assert symbols carry a content-stable NodeID and 'history()/snapshot_diff() compute added/modified/removed by NodeID set difference', with deterministic v5 UUIDs on SQLite. The actual SaveSnapshotDelta (code_intelligence_repository.go:600-695) keys carry-forward and child rows on PATH only ((repo_snapshot_id, path) / file_path / from_file). code_symbols is INSERTed with NO ON CONFLICT and NO unique key — IDs are server-assigned and CHANGE every snapshot, and there is no node_id column anywhere. So NodeID-based history/diff is unimplemented; you cannot diff two snapshots at symbol level today, and the carry-forward logic re-inserts symbols with fresh IDs so even a 'symbol moved file' or 'signature changed' diff is impossible without the new column + a stable derivation that survives line-number drift (your SymbolNodeID includes start-line via overloadIdx ambiguity but ids.go keys on path+kind+name#idx — a pure line move or rename breaks it).
issues.fix: Add node_id TEXT (indexed) to code_symbols in both schemas; make SymbolNodeID independent of volatile fields (path+kind+name+signature-hash, NOT start_line). Rewrite SaveSnapshotDelta carry-forward to match on node_id, not path, and add the UNIQUE(snapshot_id, node_id) the diff relies on. Treat 'temporal moat' as new work gated on this; the ported delta gives you per-file re-parse only, not graph diff.

issues.area: Storage — Postgres-isms bleeding into the local SQLite path
issues.problem: The carry-forward SQL the spec says both drivers implement 'verbatim' is irreducibly Postgres: NOT (path = ANY($3)) with pq.Array (repo:617,632), and every read path binds pq.Array (lines 906/918/937/949). pq.Array and ANY($n::text[]) do not exist in SQLite. The spec's mitigation ('bind changed paths as a temp table _changed') is correct in principle but is a full rewrite of every multi-path query (carry-forward x4 tables, ListSymbolsForPaths, ListEdgesForPaths, GetSymbolsByIDs), not a port. Worse: models themselves embed pq.StringArray as struct fields (devops.go:353/419/458/573 FilePaths/Labels/etc) and JSONBMap — so the 'database-agnostic graph schema, strip db tags' claim is false; pq.StringArray is a lib/pq type that won't Scan from SQLite and forces lib/pq into the local binary, breaking the build-tag isolation in the release spec.
issues.fix: Replace pq.StringArray fields with a portable JSON/TEXT-CSV custom type implementing Scan for both string and []byte. Rewrite all =ANY()/pq.Array call sites behind a driver method (e.g. driver.InClause(paths)) that emits temp-table-join on SQLite and ANY() on PG. Audit every pq.* reference; none may appear in a file compiled without the hosted tag.

issues.area: Storage — JSONBMap.Scan silently nulls on SQLite
issues.problem: JSONBMap.Scan (pulse_pod.go:77) asserts value.([]byte) and on failure does `if !ok { return nil }` — swallowing the error and leaving the map nil. lib/pq returns jsonb as []byte, so this works on Postgres. mattn/go-sqlite3 returns TEXT columns (where JSON is stored) as Go `string`, not []byte. Result: every Symbol.Metadata / File.Imports / Edge.Metadata read on the LOCAL tier silently comes back nil with no error. Since imports (devops.go:223) and symbol metadata feed import-edge extraction and impact reasons, the local graph is quietly degraded and tests that only run on PG won't catch it.
issues.fix: Make Scan handle both []byte and string (switch v:=value.(type){case []byte; case string}) and return an error on unknown type instead of nil. Add a storagecontract test that round-trips a JSONBMap through the SQLite driver specifically (the spec's shared contract test is the right place, but it must assert non-nil).

issues.area: Lexical search — code-aware tokenizer claimed as port, doesn't exist
issues.problem: Decision #2 and section 6 are built on a custom bleve analyzer that splits camelCase/snake_case (atlas_code analyzer, SplitIdentifier, edge-ngram, per-field boosts) plus a trigram side-index, described as porting 'the BM25 path'. The actual code (code_search_service.go:269) calls bleve.New(indexPath, bleve.NewIndexMapping()) — the DEFAULT mapping, no custom analyzer, no camel/snake splitting, no field boosts — and queries via bleve.NewQueryStringQuery (416). So the headline 'code-aware lexical' retrieval and the trigram index are entirely net-new. NewQueryStringQuery is also a footgun: user input like 'foo:bar' or unbalanced parens throws a parse error; a code search box must not pass raw input to the query-string parser.
issues.fix: Budget the custom analyzer + trigram index as new work (not S). Replace NewQueryStringQuery with a constructed disjunction over analyzed fields (MatchQuery per field with boosts) so arbitrary identifiers can't trip the query parser. Keep the default-mapping path only as a fallback.

issues.area: Lexical search — no ApplyDelta; full RemoveAll+rebuild every index
issues.problem: Section 6 specifies Index.ApplyDelta(added, modified, removedPaths) as 'incremental batch update — avoids full rebuild on push delta', implying it's ported. buildIndexLocked (code_search_service.go:255-264) does os.RemoveAll(indexPath) and bleve.New from scratch on EVERY rebuild — there is no incremental update path. bleve supports batch delete-by-id + re-index, but the symbol IDs change every snapshot (see delta finding), so you cannot delete the old docs by id. ApplyDelta is unimplementable until symbol IDs are stable (node_id). As specified, every 'delta' index still does a full bleve rebuild, defeating the delta performance claim for search.
issues.fix: Implement ApplyDelta only after node_id is the bleve doc id (not symbol.ID.String() which is volatile). Until then, document that search index is full-rebuild on every snapshot and remove the incremental claim, or accept O(repo) bleve rebuild on each push.

issues.area: CGO / static build — 'pure-Go static' is misleading and won't link
issues.problem: The release spec's local-static flavor (-tags purego, CGO_ENABLED=0) swaps only sqlite to modernc, but tree-sitter (go-tree-sitter + 7 grammars) is import "C"/unsafe (confirmed tree_sitter_parser.go:598) and MANDATORY in every build. With CGO_ENABLED=0 the whole module fails to compile — you cannot get a CGO-free binary that still parses. The spec half-acknowledges this in a footnote but the CI matrix (ci.yml local-static, cgo:'0') will hard-fail at build, and goreleaser sets CGO_ENABLED=1 globally so cross-compiling darwin/linux x amd64/arm64 needs a full cross C-toolchain per target (zig cc / osxcross), which the .goreleaser overrides gesture at but will be the single biggest release-eng pain. modernc sqlite under CGO_ENABLED=1 also reverts to needing care.
issues.fix: Drop the CGO_ENABLED=0 'purego' matrix cell — it cannot build with tree-sitter. Either (a) gate tree-sitter behind a build tag with a regex-only parser fallback to get a genuine static build, or (b) admit CGO is always required and invest in the goreleaser-cross/zig toolchain up front. Don't advertise 'pure-Go static binary' until (a) exists.

issues.area: MCP — token-budget blowups in graph_export and impact
issues.problem: graph_export scope:full returns 'inline {nodes,edges}' for subgraph but the tool input allows scope:full inline; a monorepo graph is 10^5+ nodes and will blow the context window if an agent calls it without scope:subgraph. The spec only converts full to a resource link in prose, not in the schema (no required root for subgraph is enforced cross-field; JSON Schema can't express 'root required iff scope=subgraph'). Similarly impact/cross_repo_impact with high max_depth over a hub symbol returns unbounded impacted_symbols/files; the _meta.truncated flag is advisory and the cross_repo_impact example shows tests_total:2641 with tests_truncated — but the actual buildCrossRepoFindings returns map[string]any (confirmed code_intelligence_service.go:1145 returns map[string]any), so the typed CrossRepoImpactResult / per-field truncation is itself net-new mapping, and nothing in the engine enforces a node cap before serialization.
issues.fix: Hard-cap every list result server-side BEFORE marshal (e.g. max 200 nodes/tools call) and require cursor paging; reject scope:full inline (force resource link) in the handler, not just docs. Convert buildCrossRepoFindings' map[string]any to a typed, capped struct as real work. Add a max-bytes guard on the MCP content block.

issues.area: Concurrency — tree-sitter parser sharing + SQLite single-writer
issues.problem: Section 11 says 'each goroutine owns its own tree-sitter Parser'. Confirmed the source creates+Closes a parser per ParseFile (tree_sitter_parser.go:38-39), which is correct, but the spec elsewhere implies a stateless reusable Parser struct — tree-sitter Parser and Tree are NOT goroutine-safe and must never be shared; the spec must make per-goroutine allocation a hard rule, and Tree.Close()/Parser.Close() are mandatory or you leak C memory under the worker pool. Separately, the SQLite single-global-write-mutex + BEGIN IMMEDIATE is correct for one process, but the spec also offers 'atlas daemon' in-process channel queue AND admits two `atlas index` invocations on one machine — two PROCESSES against one .atlas/atlas.db will still hit SQLITE_BUSY despite WAL because the mutex is per-process; busy_timeout=5000 only papers over short contention and a long full-index write will exceed it.
issues.fix: State explicitly: one tree_sitter.Parser per goroutine, Close in defer, never pooled/shared (sync.Pool of *Parser is acceptable only if Close-on-evict). For multi-process local writes, take an OS file lock on the db (flock) so a second `atlas index` blocks rather than racing; raise busy_timeout and wrap writes in retry-on-BUSY. Don't claim WAL makes concurrent writers safe — WAL allows concurrent reads with one writer, not concurrent writers.

issues.area: Effort sizing — StorageDriver interface surface is far larger than 'L'
issues.problem: The StorageDriver interface in section 3 has ~45 methods spanning repos/snapshots/graph-reads/cross-repo/test-intel/jobs, and must be implemented TWICE with byte-identical semantics (incl. carry-forward delta, claim-job atomicity, cursor stability). The Postgres impl is a lift of an existing repository, but the SQLite impl is a from-scratch reimplementation of every query with different array/temp-table idioms, different concurrency, deterministic UUID generation, and schema migrations — plus a shared contract test that proves equivalence. 'L' undercounts; this is the single biggest deliverable and gates everything downstream.
issues.fix: Split the keystone into milestones: (1) repos+snapshots+graph-reads (unblocks index/search/impact), (2) cross-repo+coverage, (3) jobs. Write the storagecontract_test.go FIRST and run it against both drivers in CI from day one (the release spec has this; make it a gate, not nightly). Budget SQLite driver as XL.

issues.area: Job queue — SQLite claim is not atomic as written
issues.problem: Section 10 claims local claim = 'UPDATE ... WHERE id=(SELECT ... LIMIT 1) RETURNING under the single write mutex'. SQLite added UPDATE...RETURNING in 3.35, but SQLite has no SELECT...FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED; correctness relies ENTIRELY on the in-process write mutex. That is fine for one process but the spec also markets a durable-ish local daemon and admits multiple processes. Across processes the SELECT-then-UPDATE is a classic TOCTOU even with RETURNING, because two processes can both read the same pending row before either commits (BEGIN IMMEDIATE serializes the writes but both can still target the same id, and the loser updates 0 rows silently — which the code must detect via RowsAffected, easy to forget).
issues.fix: For local, single-writer-process is the only safe model — enforce it with a daemon lock file and route all index jobs through it; document that direct concurrent `atlas index` processes bypass the queue. Always check RowsAffected on the claim UPDATE and loop if 0. Do not imply SKIP-LOCKED-equivalent semantics on SQLite.

issues.area: Cross-repo on local tier — false capability surface
issues.problem: The SDK/CLI/MCP specs say cross-repo 'degrades gracefully to no consumers (honest empty)' on local, and Capabilities.CrossScope=false. But the storage interface still requires SaveRoutes/SaveCrossDeps/ListProducerRoutes on the SQLite driver, and analysis runs route/crossservice analyzers on every index regardless of tier — so local pays the full analysis cost and stores routes it can never match across repos. More subtly, a local user who indexes two sibling folders into ONE .atlas db COULD match cross-repo, but the spec hard-codes CrossScope=false and 404s the op, contradicting the 'cross-repo analysis is Apache/works locally if multiple folders indexed' claim in the licensing section.
issues.fix: Pick one: either local single-db multi-repo cross-matching is supported (then CrossScope is about webhooks/org-scale, not capability) or it's hosted-only (then drop the licensing claim that it works locally). Make analyzers skippable on local to avoid paying for unusable data, or keep them but be consistent about whether ListProducerRoutes scans across repo_ids in one local db.

issues.area: SDK — Job[T] generics + agentic ops on local
issues.problem: pkg/atlas exposes RCA/Fix/Review returning *Job[RCAResult] etc., but those live in internal/cloud (BUSL, //go:build hosted). If the public Engine interface in the Apache-licensed pkg/atlas references RCAResult/FixResult types, those result types must also live outside internal/cloud (Apache) while their implementations are hosted-only — otherwise the local Apache build won't compile the interface. The spec puts the interface methods in the public facade but the result/runner in BUSL hosted, which creates a build-tag split inside one interface (methods present in the type but unimplemented locally → must return ErrTierUnsupported, meaning the interface is partially a lie on local).
issues.fix: Keep all DTO result types in pkg/apitypes (Apache, always compiled); keep only runners under internal/cloud. The Engine interface compiles everywhere; local impls of RCA/Fix/Review return ErrTierUnsupported. Verify the Apache-only build (no hosted tag) compiles pkg/atlas with these methods — add it to CI.
